units.unit_name: Curricular Goals and Competencies - Matter, Physical World, Living World
topics: CG 1 – Explores the world of matter, its interactions, and properties at the atomic level

C 1.1 – Describes classification of elements in the Periodic Table, and explains how compounds (including carbon compounds) are formed based on the atomic structure (Bohr's model) and properties (valency).

C 1.2 – Investigates the nature and properties of chemical substances (distillation, crystallisation, chromatography, centrifugation, types and properties of mixtures, solutions, colloids, and suspensions)
C 1.3 – Describes and represents chemical interactions and changes using symbols and chemical equations (acid and base, metal and non-metal, reversible and irreversible)

units.unit_name: Curricular Goals and Competencies - Physical World Principles
topics: CG 2 – Explores the physical world around them, and understands scientific principles and laws based on observations and analysis

C 2.1 – Applies Newton's laws to explain the effect of forces (change in state of motion — displacement and direction, velocity and acceleration, uniform circular motion, acceleration due to gravity) and analyses graphical and mathematical representations of motion in one dimension
C 2.2 – Explains the relationship between mass and weight using universal law of gravitation, and connect it to the laws of motion

C 2.3 – Manipulates the position of object and properties of lenses (focus, centre of curvature) to observe image characteristics and correspondence with a ray diagram, and extends this understanding to a combination of lenses (telescope, microscope)

C 2.4 – Manipulates and analyses different characteristics of the circuit (current, voltage, resistance) and mathematises their relationship (Ohm's law), and applies it to everyday usage (electricity bill, short circuit, safety measures)
C 2.5 – Defines work in scientific terms, and represents the relationship between potential and kinetic energy (conservation of energy) in mathematical expressions
C 2.6 – Demonstrates the principle of mechanical advantage by constructing simple machines (system of levers and pulleys)
C 2.7 – Describes the origin and properties of sound (wavelength, frequency, amplitude) and differences in what we hear as it propagates through different instruments

C 2.8 – Explores interconnected systems and phenomena that support and affect life on Earth (hydrosphere, biosphere, atmosphere, geosphere, cryosphere and their interrelationships, earth processes, hazards, etc.)
key_concepts: Earth systems (hydrosphere, biosphere, atmosphere, geosphere, cryosphere)

sourceSessionPlan.title: Refraction

teacherLessonNotes.sessionOverview: This session introduces the fundamental concept of refraction in Class X Science. Students will explore how light changes direction when passing between media of different optical densities (e.g., air to glass or water). The lesson focuses on defining refraction, observing real-world examples like a bent straw in water, and understanding the underlying cause: change in speed.
prerequisiteKnowledge: Light travels in straight lines (rectilinear propagation)
Understanding that light is an electromagnetic wave
previousSessionRecap: Review of how light behaves as a ray
Distinction between transparent, translucent, and opaque objects
Concept of the normal line at a surface interface
learningOutcomes: Explain that refraction occurs due to change in speed of light in different media.
Distinguish between rarer and denser optical media based on bending direction.
Observe and describe the apparent shift in position of objects submerged in water.

teachingPlan.teachingStrategy: Direct explanation with analogy (car on road)
Board work with step-by-step diagram construction
Quick fire questions and exit ticket

lessonPurpose: To bridge the gap between abstract wave theory and observable physical phenomena.
To establish the foundational rules required for understanding lenses later in the chapter.
teachingSequence: 1. Hook: Show a pencil partially submerged in water; ask 'Is it actually bent?'
2. Define Refraction as the bending of light at an interface.
3. Explain the cause: Light speed changes (fast in air, slow in glass/water). Use the car analogy (wheels hitting mud/sand change direction).
4. Demonstrate with a laser pointer through a water tank or glass slab to show bending towards/away from normal.
5. Draw ray diagrams on board: Air -> Glass (bends towards normal), Glass -> Air (bends away from normal).
6. Discuss the 'Apparent Depth' concept briefly as an application.
guidedPractice: Ask students to predict what happens if light enters a denser medium at 90 degrees.
Have students draw the path of a ray passing from water to air on their notebooks.
Check understanding by asking why stars twinkle (introductory thought).

lessonBlocks.title: Introduction
teacherPrompt: Look at this glass of water with a straw in it. Does the straw look straight or bent?
Why do you think our eyes are being tricked?
explanation: The light rays coming from the submerged part of the straw travel through water (denser) and then air (rarer).
As they exit, their speed changes, causing them to bend.
Our brain assumes light travels in a straight line, so it projects the image along the extended path, making the object appear shifted.
examples: A coin at the bottom of a bowl appears raised when water is poured into it.
Fish underwater appearing closer to the surface than they actually are.

lessonBlocks.title: Concept Development & Rules
teacherPrompt: What happens to light when it enters glass from air?
Does it bend towards or away from the normal?
explanation: Refraction is defined as the bending of a ray of light when it passes obliquely from one transparent medium into another.
Rule 1: When light travels from Rarer (Air) to Denser (Glass/Water), it bends TOWARDS the normal. Speed decreases.
Rule 2: When light travels from Denser (Water/Glass) to Rarer (Air), it bends AWAY FROM the normal. Speed increases.
examples: Laser beam entering a glass slab.
Sun appearing earlier than actual sunrise due to atmospheric refraction.
boardWork: Write: Rule 1 (Rare -> Dense): Bends Towards Normal
Write: Rule 2 (Dense -> Rare): Bends Away from Normal
boardSteps: Step 1: Draw a horizontal interface line.
Step 3: Draw incident ray in air hitting at an angle. Draw refracted ray bending towards normal inside glass.
proofSteps: State that speed of light is maximum in vacuum/air.
Apply Snell's Law concept qualitatively (n1 sin i = n2 sin r).

conceptFlow.definition: The change in direction of a wave passing from one medium to another caused by its change in speed.
conceptFlow.coreExplanation: Light travels at different speeds in different media. When it crosses the boundary obliquely, this difference in speed causes the light ray to bend. This is not reflection; the light enters the new medium but changes path.
conceptFlow.importance: It explains natural phenomena like twinkling stars, mirages, and is essential for understanding how lenses (glasses, cameras) work.
observedIn: Bent appearance of a straw in water

conceptFlow.relationshipWithPrevious: Builds on the concept that light travels in straight lines (rectilinear propagation).
conceptFlow.relationshipWithFuture: Directly leads to understanding Lenses, Focal Length, Lens Formula, and Human Eye defects.
teacherMoves: Emphasize the word 'obliquely' - normal incidence does not cause bending.
examples: Car moving from road to mud: one wheel hits first, causing turn.
Laser pointer through a glass of water with milk (to make path visible).
visuals: Ray diagram showing incident and refracted rays.

commonMisconceptionsDetailed.misconception: Students think light bends because it 'hits' the surface and bounces off slightly like a ball hitting a wall at an angle.
commonMisconceptionsDetailed.correction: Clarify that refraction is due to change in speed, not collision. Use the car analogy where wheels slow down gradually or one side slows first.

commonMisconceptionsDetailed.misconception: Students believe light always bends towards the normal regardless of direction.
commonMisconceptionsDetailed.correction: Reiterate: Towards Normal (Rare->Dense), Away from Normal (Dense->Rare). Use a mnemonic like 'Towards' for 'Thicker/Denser'.

sourceSessionPlan.introduction: Imagine you are looking at a fish swimming in an aquarium. Does it look like it's exactly where it is? Or does it seem closer to the glass than it really is? Today, we will uncover why objects underwater appear shifted and learn about 'Refraction'—the bending of light that tricks our eyes every day.

theory.overview: Refraction is the phenomenon where a ray of light changes its path (bends) when passing obliquely from one transparent medium to another. This occurs because light travels at different speeds in different media. It does not occur if the light enters normally (perpendicularly).
keyPoints: Refraction is caused by a change in the speed of light.
Light bends towards the normal when entering a denser medium from a rarer one.
Light bends away from the normal when entering a rarer medium from a denser one.
theory.detailedContent: When light travels from air (a rarer medium) into glass or water (denser media), it slows down. This change in speed causes the wavefront to pivot, resulting in bending towards the normal line drawn at the point of incidence. Conversely, when light exits a denser medium like glass back into air, it speeds up and bends away from the normal. A common example is a straw placed in a glass of water; the part under water appears bent or raised because the light rays coming from that part bend as they leave the water to enter our eyes.

activities.name: The Bent Straw Experiment
instructions: Fill a clear glass tumbler with about half water.
Place a straight pencil or straw into the water so that part of it is submerged and visible from the side.
Ask students to observe the position of the pencil at the water surface level.
Discuss why the pencil appears bent or broken at the interface.

activities.name: Laser in Water Tank
instructions: Fill a rectangular glass tank with water and add a few drops of milk to make it slightly turbid.
Shine a laser pointer through the side of the tank so the beam enters obliquely into the water.
Observe the path of the light ray inside the water compared to its path in air.
Draw the observed path on the board and label 'Incident Ray' and 'Refracted Ray'.

assets.altText: A glowing laser shines forth from the European Southern Observatory’s Very Large Telescope. Piercing the dark Chilean skies, its mission is to help astronomers explore the far reaches of the cosmos. ESO Photo Ambassador Gerhard Hüdepohl was on hand to capture the moment in a stunning portrait of modern science in action. We have all gazed up at the night sky and seen the stars gently twinkle as the Earth’s turbulent atmosphere causes their light to shimmer. This is undoubtedly a beautiful sight, but it causes problems for astronomers, who want the crispest possible views. To help them achieve this, professional stargazers use something that sounds as though it has come from science fiction: a laser guide star that creates an artificial star 90 km above the surface of the Earth. The method by which it achieves this is nothing short of remarkable. The laser energises sodium atoms high in the Earth’s mesosphere, causing them to glow and creating a bright dot that to observers on the ground appears to be a man-made star. Observations of how this “star” twinkles are fed into the Very Large Telescope’s adaptive optics system, controlling a deformable mirror in the telescope to restore the image of the star to a sharp point. By doing this, the system also compensates for the distorting effect of the atmosphere in the region around the artificial star. The end result is an exceptionally crisp view of the sky, allowing ESO astronomers to make stunning observations of the Universe, almost as though the VLT were above the atmosphere in space.
